meta.clinicianNotes: Chronic cholesteatoma picture — longstanding unilateral discharge and progressive conductive loss she's neglected for years. Learning objective: students should pick up on the chronicity and foul-smelling discharge as red flags distinct from simple otitis media, and be sensitive about why she delayed seeking care. Common student issue: not asking about smell/character of discharge or duration, accepting 'ear infections' at face value.

patient.name: Mei-Lin Tan
patient.additionalNotes: Mei-Lin is a little guarded about why she's left this so long — she's been busy running her restaurant and kept putting it off. She may initially minimise how long it's been going on and needs gentle, non-judgemental follow-up questions to get the full picture.

history.reasonForAppointment: Persistent left ear discharge and hearing loss that she's finally had time to get checked, after years of on-and-off symptoms.

hearing.declineDetails: Left ear hearing has been gradually worsening over several years — she initially says 'a couple of years' but if pressed on timeline admits it's probably been more like 5-6 years, on and off. Right ear normal. Left ear now quite muffled most of the time.

earHealth.details: Recurrent left ear discharge for years, comes and goes, sometimes with an unpleasant smell she's embarrassed to mention unless asked directly. Occasional mild ache, usually manageable with over-the-counter drops which give brief relief. Has never actually seen a doctor about it before — always assumed it was 'just an ear infection' that would clear up.

generalHealth.medications: None regularly. Uses OTC ear drops when the discharge flares up.

noiseHistory.details: Works in a restaurant kitchen — busy but not loud in the industrial sense. No significant noise exposure.

history.otherConcerns: Mei-Lin is a bit embarrassed she's left it this long and worried she'll be told off for not coming in sooner. Wants to know if it can be fixed and whether it's serious.
